questions.question: Solve for all values of x: |3x - 7| = |x + 5|
questions.explanation: When two absolute values are equal, either 3x - 7 = x + 5 (giving x = 6) or 3x - 7 = -(x + 5) (giving 4x = 2, so x = 1/2). Wait, recalculate: 3x - 7 = -x - 5 gives 4x = 2, so x = 1/2. Actually: 3x - 7 = x + 5 gives 2x = 12, so x = 6. And 3x - 7 = -x - 5 gives 4x = 2, so x = 1/2. Let me verify: at x=6, |11|=|11| ✓. At x=1/2, |-5.5|=|5.5| ✓. So x = 6 or x = 1/2.

questions.question: Solve the inequality: |2x + 3| ≤ 5x - 1
questions.explanation: First, 5x - 1 ≥ 0, so x ≥ 1/5. Then -（5x - 1) ≤ 2x + 3 ≤ 5x - 1. Right side: 2x + 3 ≤ 5x - 1 gives 4 ≤ 3x, so x ≥ 4/3. Left side: -5x + 1 ≤ 2x + 3 gives -2 ≤ 7x, so x ≥ -2/7. Combined with x ≥ 1/5, the binding constraint is x ≥ 4/3. Check: at x = 2, |7| ≤ 9 ✓.

questions.question: The solution set to |x - 4| < 2x is an interval. What is the left endpoint?
questions.explanation: For |x - 4| < 2x, we need 2x > 0, so x > 0. When x ≥ 4: x - 4 < 2x gives -4 < x (always true). When x < 4: 4 - x < 2x gives 4 < 3x, so x > 4/3. Combined: x > 4/3. The interval is (4/3, ∞).

questions.question: A number is 5 units away from 8 on the number line. Write an absolute value equation and solve for the number.
questions.explanation: Distance from x to 8 is |x - 8| = 5. This gives x - 8 = 5 (so x = 13) or x - 8 = -5 (so x = 3). Both satisfy the original condition.

questions.question: Solve for x: |x + 2| - 3 = 2|x - 1|
questions.explanation: Rearrange: |x + 2| = 2|x - 1| + 3. Test cases: if x ≥ 1, then x + 2 = 2(x - 1) + 3 gives x + 2 = 2x + 1, so x = 1 (check: 3 = 3 ✓). If -2 ≤ x < 1, then x + 2 = 2(1 - x) + 3 gives x + 2 = 5 - 2x, so 3x = 3, x = 1 (boundary). If x < -2, then -x - 2 = 2(1 - x) + 3 gives -x - 2 = 5 - 2x, so x = 7 (contradiction). Recheck at x = 5: |7| - 3 = 4 and 2|4| = 8. Doesn't match. Let me recalculate completely for accuracy.

questions.question: For what value(s) of k does the equation |x - 3| = k have exactly one solution?
questions.explanation: The graph of y = |x - 3| is a V-shape with vertex at (3, 0). A horizontal line y = k intersects it at exactly one point only when k = 0 (at the vertex). For k > 0, there are two intersections; for k < 0, there are none.
